items.text: Family history: asthma, allergy, COPD in family
Duration and nature of symptoms: dyspnea, wheezing, chest tightness, cough
Timing: at rest / during exercise / post-exercise / at night
Triggers: cold, dust, animals, odors, exercise, infections
Allergies: pollen, animal, other — physician-confirmed?
Smoking / snus / e-cigarettes / vaping
Work environment: dust, gases, fumes (occupational asthma screening)
Comorbidities: allergic rhinitis, nasal polyps, reflux, obesity, sleep apnea, depression, arrhythmia
Medications: current asthma drugs (maintenance + PRN), name them; NSAIDs / beta-blockers?
Asthma control: weekly symptoms, night symptoms, reliever use, functional capacity
Exacerbation history: OCS courses, ED visits, hospitalizations in past 12 months
Spirometry + bronchodilator test (FEV₁/FVC, ≥12% and 200 mL improvement)
PEF monitoring 2 weeks (≥20% and 60 L/min diurnal variation OR ≥15% bronchodilator response)
Consider bronchial provocation: methacholine (PD₂₀FEV₁ ≤0.6 mg), mannitol, exercise test
FeNO: >25 ppb suggestive of eosinophilic inflammation; >50 ppb strongly supports; <25 ppb less likely
Blood eosinophils (≥300 cells/µL supports T2-high phenotype; guides biologic selection)
Allergy screening / prick tests / specific IgE panel
Chest X-ray (if indicated: first presentation, atypical features, poor treatment response)
Blood tests: CBC, CRP, total IgE (if indicated)

tables.caption: Treatment Steps (GINA 2024 Track 1 — preferred)
rows: As-needed low-dose ICS-formoterol (preferred); SABA-only no longer recommended alone
High-dose ICS-formoterol + add-on LAMA (tiotropium); refer for T2 phenotyping
Specialist-led: biologic therapy by phenotype (see below)

tables.caption: Biologic Therapy by Phenotype (Step 5)
rows: Allergic asthma, elevated total IgE, positive allergy tests
Eosinophilic: blood eos ≥300/µL, rapid eosinophil depletion
T2-high: eos ≥150/µL OR FeNO ≥25 ppb; also for comorbid CRSwNP/eczema
Severe asthma across phenotypes (T2-high and T2-low)

items.text: Daytime symptoms ≤2×/week → well controlled
No night waking due to asthma
No activity limitation
Reliever use ≤2×/week (SABA or as-needed ICS-formoterol)
ACT score: ≥20 well controlled, 16–19 partly, ≤15 uncontrolled
Well controlled ≥3 months → consider step-down: reduce ICS dose by 25–50%
Risk factors for exacerbation: high SABA use, poor adherence, no ICS, low FEV₁, smoking, eosinophilia, obesity
Exercise symptoms: cough, dyspnea, wheezing during or after exercise
Cold air exposure: symptoms when training in cold
Training volume: hours/week, sports, endurance/strength split %
Respiratory infections and sick days last season
EILO screening: laryngeal symptoms at rest/exercise/after (Newcastle questionnaire)
Breathing devices: WellO₂, Sarvikuono, Airtrim, Lungplus
Consider EVH test or mannitol test (eucapnic voluntary hyperventilation); higher specificity for athletes
Exercise challenge: treadmill or field test with serial spirometry
Reflux symptoms: burning, belching, acid taste (common in athlete cough)
Vocal cord dysfunction / ILO: consider CLE (continuous laryngoscopy during exercise) if EILO suspected
Return-to-sport: symptom-free, medication optimized, PEF stable, anti-doping check (TUE if needed)
Prednisolone 20–40 mg/day for 5–7 days (no taper needed for short courses)
Increase reliever: SABA 4–10 puffs q20min first hour, or ICS-formoterol as needed up to 72 µg/day formoterol
Check and correct inhaler technique
Assess and address adherence barriers
Identify and manage triggers (infection, allergen, NSAID, beta-blocker)
Follow-up visit in 2–4 weeks; step-up maintenance if ≥2 exacerbations/year
Inhaler technique: teach, demonstrate, check — repeat at every visit
Self-monitoring instructions (PEF diary, symptom diary, ACT questionnaire)
Written asthma action plan (green/yellow/red zones — when to step up, when to seek care)
Medication reimbursement (Kela B-statement for asthma drugs)
Comorbidity management: allergic rhinitis (treat the unified airway), reflux, obesity
Follow-up appointment booked (3 months after starting/changing treatment, then annually if stable)
